summary:
The Edo Period (1603–1868 AD) was the era of the [[event:2336|Tokugawa Shogunate]], named for the capital city of Edo (modern Tokyo), during which Japan experienced a unique combination of prolonged peace (the Pax Tokugawa), controlled isolation from the outside world ([[event:2418|Sakoku]]), and the remarkable development of a sophisticated urban popular culture that produced some of Japan's most distinctive artistic and literary traditions. With the resolution of the Sengoku (warring states) period through [[event:3308|Tokugawa Ieyasu's]] consolidation, Japan enjoyed over 250 years without major internal warfare — an unprecedented period of stability that allowed commerce, crafts, and cities to flourish under conditions of peace.

Edo itself became one of the world's largest cities — home to over one million people by the 18th century — generating a vibrant merchant (chonin) culture that patronized [[event:3309|kabuki theater]], [[event:3310|ukiyo-e woodblock prints]] (Hiroshige, Hokusai), haiku poetry, comic fiction (gesaku), and elaborate ceremonial culture. The tea ceremony, flower arranging, and noh theater received their classical codifications during this period. Agricultural productivity increased through land reclamation and improved techniques; the population grew from roughly 12 million to 30 million by the late Edo period. The [[event:2418|Sakoku]] policy channeled the limited foreign trade through Nagasaki (for Dutch and Chinese merchants) and maintained cultural insularity that preserved Japanese distinctiveness — but also left Japan without access to the military and technological advances that Western nations were accumulating. When Commodore Perry's Black Ships arrived in 1853 demanding trade, Japan's technological gap with the industrializing West had become alarming, leading to the political crisis and Meiji Restoration of 1868.
